source.title: Directed evolution of a filamentous fungus for thermotolerance

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to develop thermotolerant strains of the entomopathogenic fungus Metarhizium anisopliae to overcome its limited growth at elevated temperatures (upper limit ~32°C), which hinders its use as a biocontrol agent in hot climates and against insects exhibiting behavioral fever. Traditional genetic engineering is inadequate for complex phenotypes like thermotolerance. The hypothesis was that directed evolution using a novel continuous culture device (Evolugator™) could select for thermotolerant variants without a priori knowledge of required mutations.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: The study used the Evolugator™ continuous culture method to select thermotolerant M. anisopliae variants. Over 4 months and 22 cycles, two variants (EVG016, EVG017) were isolated with robust growth at 36.5°C; EVG017 grew at 37°C. Insect bioassays showed retained pathogenicity but altered virulence parameters. This is the first use of continuous culture to select for complex phenotypes like thermotolerance in a filamentous fungus.
process_signals: Initial problem: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance, limiting biocontrol efficacy.
Method: Evolugator™ enables long-term continuous culture and selection.
Key result: Two thermotolerant variants obtained with different growth limits.
Validation: Insect bioassays confirmed pathogenicity retention.
metrics: LD50 and ST50 in insect bioassays
failures_or_limits: Only one variant grew at 37°C; no growth at 38°C.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Filamentous fungi are important biocatalysts, but traditional genetic engineering is slow and often fails for complex phenotypes like thermotolerance. Continuous culture can harness natural selection, but existing methods (serial dilution, chemostats) have limitations (contamination, wall growth). The Evolugator™ overcomes these by using flexible tubing replaced each cycle, enabling long-term adaptation. M. anisopliae is a promising biocontrol agent limited by low thermotolerance (upper limit 32°C). The goal was to adapt strain ARSEF2575 to grow at 37°C as a proof-of-principle.
process_signals: Problem: Need for improved fungal strains; traditional methods inadequate.
Constraint: Complex phenotypes require genome-wide changes.
Hypothesis: Directed evolution via Evolugator™ can yield thermotolerant strains.
Target: Adapt M. anisopliae from 32°C to 37°C.
methods: Evolugator™ continuous culture (described in de Crécy et al., 2007)
failures_or_limits: Serial dilution: contamination, cannot handle aggregates.
Chemostats: wall growth limits long-term adaptation.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Selection of thermostable M. anisopliae isolates
section_gists.gist: M. anisopliae was cultured in the Evolugator™ starting at 28°C. Over 22 cycles in 4 months, temperature was gradually increased. Growth was monitored by OD; dilution cycles were triggered by OD or duration. Rapid growth in cycles 1–4 (28–30°C). From cycle 5, growth slowed, and maximal OD dropped at 31–32°C, indicating decreased fitness. Temperature was stabilized at certain points to allow fit variants to dominate. Two strains, EVG016 (cycle 18) and EVG017 (cycle 22), were isolated. ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing confirmed they were derivatives of the wild type.
process_signals: Initial temperature: 28°C.
Observation: Growth slowed and OD dropped at higher temperatures, requiring longer cycles and temperature stabilization.
Adaptation strategy: Allow fit variants to take over before increasing temperature.
Isolation: EVG016 from cycle 18, EVG017 from cycle 22.
Validation: Genetic markers confirmed strain identity.
methods: ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing for strain verification
failures_or_limits: Decreased fitness at 31–32°C (low OD, long cycles).
Need to stabilize temperature to allow adaptation.

section_gists.heading: Phenotypic characterization of M. anisopliae thermostable isolates
section_gists.gist: EVG016 and EVG017 were characterized. EVG016 had reduced green pigmentation; EVG017 produced white colonies with severely reduced sporulation (<1% of wild type). Conidial production on solid substrate: EVG016 7.7×10^11 vs wild type 3.9×10^12 conidia/kg; EVG017 <1%. A variant EVG017g was later isolated that retained thermotolerance and restored sporulation. In liquid culture at 28°C, all strains grew similarly. At 36.5°C, only EVG016 and EVG017 grew; at 37°C, only EVG017 grew; no growth at 38°C. On solid agar at 36–37°C, none showed radial growth, but remained viable. Microscopy: EVG017 formed appressoria more rapidly.
process_signals: Trade-off: Thermotolerance associated with reduced sporulation, especially EVG017.
Partial result: EVG016 less affected in sporulation.
Revision: Passage through insect to restore sporulation (EVG017g).
Growth limit: Narrow threshold; no growth at 38°C.
Phenotype: Liquid vs solid growth differences.
methods: Solid substrate fermentation for conidial counts
Bioscreen C for liquid growth kinetics
metrics: OD over time at various temperatures
failures_or_limits: No radial growth on solid agar at high temperature.

section_gists.heading: Insect bioassays
section_gists.gist: Bioassays against grasshoppers (Melanoplus sanguinipes) were performed. EVG017 had insufficient spores, so it was passaged through an insect to obtain EVG017g with restored sporulation. Topical 5-dose assays showed both EVG016 and EVG017g had higher LD50 (lower infectivity) than wild type, but LD95 differences were less pronounced. ST50: EVG017g killed faster (5.5 d) than wild type (7 d); EVG016 also faster (6 d). At 36°C, no strain caused mortality, but all remained viable and killed when shifted to 28°C.
process_signals: Constraint: EVG017 sporulation too low for direct bioassay.
Solution: Passage through host to restore sporulation (EVG017g).
Result: Thermotolerant strains retained pathogenicity but with altered virulence.
Observation: Faster kill (ST50) but lower infectivity (LD50).
Thermotolerance did not enable pathogenicity at 36°C.
failures_or_limits: EVG017g passage may have affected virulence.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The Evolugator™ overcomes limitations of serial dilution and chemostats for long-term adaptation of filamentous fungi. This is the first report of directed adaptation of a filamentous fungus for a complex phenotype via continuous culture. Thermotolerant isolates showed complex changes in non-selected traits: EVG016 had decreased infectivity (LD50) but faster kill (ST50); EVG017 had severe sporulation defect but could be recovered. The genetic basis of thermotolerance is unknown; multiple mutational pathways may exist. The technology can be applied to other fungi and conditions.
process_signals: Achievement: First successful directed evolution of thermotolerance in a filamentous fungus.
Trade-offs: Sporulation and virulence altered; not necessarily linked to thermotolerance.
Uncertainty: Unknown mutations; need more isolates to study pathways.
Potential: Broad applicability of Evolugator™.
failures_or_limits: Possible attenuation from long-term liquid culture.

section_gists.heading: Conclusion
section_gists.gist: Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using the Evolugator™. The selection had no prior assumptions about required mutations. Mutants showed complex phenotypes but pathogenicity could be retained. Future work: generate designer biocontrol strains, apply to other biotechnological problems (e.g., biofuel production).
process_signals: Summary: Proof-of-principle achieved.
Future direction: Optimize strains for specific conditions.
Broader impact: Technology applicable to other eukaryotic microorganisms.
failures_or_limits: Strains not yet optimized for field use.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Isolation of thermotolerant M. anisopliae and determination of growth kinetics
section_gists.gist: The Evolugator™ uses flexible silicone tubing as a growth chamber, subdivided by gates. Medium and tubing surface are replaced each dilution cycle, preventing wall growth and contamination. Dilution can be triggered by time or OD. Two turbidimeters monitor OD. For M. anisopliae, tubing was filled with SAB medium, inoculated, and temperature controlled. Growth kinetics were measured in a Bioscreen C plate reader. Microscopy used confocal with DIC.
process_signals: Key design: Tubing replacement avoids wall growth.
Operation: Turbidostat/chemostat modes.
Setup: SAB medium, 28°C start, OD monitoring.
methods: Evolugator™ with silicone tubing, gates, peristaltic movement
failures_or_limits: Proprietary technology; full details in separate publication.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The Evolugator™ enables long-term continuous culture of filamentous fungi by periodically replacing the growth surface, thus selecting for fast-growing variants.
Gradual temperature increase with stabilization periods allows accumulation of multiple mutations required for thermotolerance.
Thermotolerance may trade off with sporulation and virulence, but these traits can be recovered or maintained.
The genetic basis of thermotolerance is likely polygenic and may involve multiple pathways.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Are the trade-offs in sporulation and virulence directly linked to thermotolerance or due to other adaptations?
How many different mutational pathways can lead to thermotolerance?
Can thermotolerance be achieved without affecting virulence?
Why did EVG017 lose sporulation so severely, and how did passage through the insect restore it?
What is the molecular mechanism of the narrow temperature threshold (37°C vs 38°C)?

events.summary: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance, limiting biocontrol efficacy.
Directed evolution via Evolugator™ can select for thermotolerant variants.
M. anisopliae was cultured in Evolugator™ starting at 28°C, temperature gradually increased over 22 cycles.
Temperature stabilized to allow fit variants to dominate before further increase.
Two thermotolerant variants EVG016 and EVG017 isolated from cycles 18 and 22.
ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing confirmed isolates were derivatives of wild type.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, and start of results, providing key problem, hypothesis, design, and early experimental events.

events.summary: Growth curves at 36.5°C and 37°C were measured for wild-type and adapted isolates using a Bioscreen C plate reader.
numbers_or_metrics: at least 30 independent growth curves each for three independent batches

events.summary: EVG017 was passaged through an insect host to restore sporulation, yielding strain EVG017g with satisfactory sporulation and retained thermotolerance.
None of the strains caused mortality at 36°C, but remained viable and killed when shifted to 28°C.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed phenotypic characterization, insect bioassays, and discussion of trade-offs and limitations.

events.summary: Observation that selection for faster growing variants appears stepwise, but may reflect changes in mutant frequency in the population.
Interpretation that thermal barriers at 32°C, 36.5°C, and 37.5°C may require multiple or complex mutations.
Uncertainty whether observed thermal barriers are concrete or an artifact of the specific evolutionary pathway taken.
Future plan to compare genome sequences of thermotolerant variants with wild type to identify genetic changes.
Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using the Evolugator, with no a priori assumptions about required mutations.
Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes in non-selected traits like conidiation and virulence, but these could be retained or recovered.
Future goal to generate designer biocontrol strains more efficient than wild type for specific conditions.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion of evolutionary dynamics, thermal barriers, future sequencing plans, and conclusions about successful adaptation and complex phenotypes.
This chunk contains only the reference list and a promotional section for BioMed Central. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: the relatively low tolerance of this species to abiotic stresses such as heat, with most strains displaying little to no growth between 35–37°C

we used a newly developed automated continuous culture method called the Evolugator™, which takes advantage of a natural selection-adaptation strategy, to select for thermotolerant variants of M. anisopliae strain 2575 displaying robust growth at 37°C
Figure 1 presents a detailed description of 22 successive selection cycles over a 4 month period
In cycles 8 and 9, the chamber temperature was not varied significantly in order to allow variants that can grow rapidly at this temperature to take over the population
Two strains, termed EVG016 and EVG017 were isolated from cells cultured in cycles 18 and 22, respectively
Sequencing of the ITS1 and a fragment of the M. anisopliae specific protease Pr1 genes revealed that both isolates were derivatives of the original wild type strain
Growth curves at 36.5°C (A) and 37°C (B) of wild-type and temperature adapted M. anisopliae isolates.

Growth experiments were performed using a high-throughput Bioscreen C plate reader and are the averages of at least 30 independent growth curves each for three independent batches of fungal conidia (>90 cultures).

Due to the reduced sporulation of EVG017, not enough spores could be directly harvested for insect bioassays. Therefore, the strain was passaged once through M. sanguinipes by rubbing the abdomen of host insects on an agar culture of EVG017.
None of the strains were pathogenic or able to cause mortality in hosts at 36°C.

However, when insects infected at 36°C were subsequently placed at 28°C, the hosts were rapidly killed by all three fungal strains, indicating that the wild type and adapted strains remained viable at 36°C, but could not cause pathogenicity and death.

the selection for faster growing variants was rapid and took roughly the same amount of time. However, at certain temperatures (32°C, 36.5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers

at certain temperatures (32°C, 36.5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers, perhaps requiring multiple or complex mutations to arise in the population.
More experiments are needed to determine if these temperatures represent concrete barriers or if they are an artifact of the peculiar evolutionary pathway taken in our experiment.
It will also be important to compare the genome sequence of the thermotolerant variants with that of wild type to determine the genetic changes involved in the acquisition of thermotolerance.

We report the successful directed adaption of a eukaryotic organism, namely the filamentous fungus, M. anisopliae, for increased thermotolerance using a novel continuous culture machine whose operation is fundamentally different from chemostats and other current methods.

Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes with respect to non-selected attributes such as conidiation and virulence, although both parameters could be retained or recovered while maintaining the thermotolerant phenotype.

The immediate goal of future research will be to generate "designer" strains of entomopathogenic fungi that are more efficient biocontrol agents than currently used wild type strains for specific insect infestations and defined environmental conditions.

After 8–10 days the whole cultures were then transferred to kraft paper bags in which they were dried for 10 days at 23–25°C, to a final moisture of < 0.4 Water Activity Units (< 6% gravimetric moisture).
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.6°C) and 20 (38°C).

Moreover, the maximal cell yield (OD) dropped significantly during cycles 7 ( $31^{\circ}$ C) and 8 ( $32^{\circ}$ C), even though cells were allowed to grow for over 200 hours each time, indicating decreased overall fitness.

Despite their confirmed thermotolerance, the adapted variants did not show increased virulence at 36–37°C, indicating that the ability to grow in vitro at 36–37°C does not necessarily mean that in vivo growth and pathogenesis will occur.

Remarkably, the resulting variant, EVG017g, maintained thermotolerance after passage through the insect and showed increased virulence compared to the non-insect passaged parent strain as measured by $ST_{50}$ .
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.
Both variants displayed robust growth at 36.5°C, whereas only one was able to grow at 37°C.

Thus, for a given mutation with a frequency of 1/N, where N is the number of cells in the population, the ability of the observer to detect an overall population of cells with the altered growth rate may appear to be stepwise although it is likely to be a reflection of changes in the frequency of the mutant in the population, since it takes as long to change from a frequency of 1/1000 to 1/1
We isolated a variant of EVG017, named EVG017g, that retained thermotolerance but was as capable of conidiation as wild type (See below).

Assays confirmed that thermotolerant variants were, indeed, entomopathogenic, albeit with complex alterations in virulence parameters such as lethal dose responses ( $LD_{50}$ ) and median survival times ( $ST_{50}$ ).
Results: Over a 4 month time course, 22 cycles of growth and dilution were used to select 2 thermotolerant variants of M.
In cycles 8 and 9, the chamber temperature was not varied significantly in order to allow variants that can grow rapidly at this temperature to take over the population.

Since both the tubing and medium is changed with every dilution cycle, the Evolugator $^{™}$ can, in principle, allow the growth and selection of fast-growing variants of filamentous fungal cells that could use the tubing as a solid surface upon which to grow $[6]$ .

The only practical way to alter such complex phenotypes is using continuous culture to select for genetic variants that can adapt to gradual changes in environmental conditions – in essence, harnessing the power of evolution by natural selection $[5]$ .

event_graph.paper_main_problem: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance (upper limit ~32°C), limiting its use as a biocontrol agent in hot climates and against insects exhibiting behavioral fever.

canonical_events.summary: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance, limiting biocontrol efficacy.
Directed evolution via Evolugator™ can select for thermotolerant variants without a priori knowledge of required mutations.
Evolugator™ continuous culture setup: flexible silicone tubing replaced each cycle to prevent wall growth, turbidostat/chemostat modes, OD monitoring at 680 nm.
M. anisopliae cultured in Evolugator™ starting at 28°C; temperature gradually increased over 22 cycles in 4 months.
Growth slowed and maximal OD dropped at 31–32°C, indicating decreased fitness; cycle duration increased.
Temperature stabilized at certain points (e.g., cycles 8–9 at ~32°C) to allow fit variants to dominate before further increase.
Two thermotolerant variants EVG016 (cycle 18) and EVG017 (cycle 22) isolated.
ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing confirmed EVG016 and EVG017 are derivatives of wild type.
Growth curves at 36.5°C and 37°C measured: both variants grew at 36.5°C; only EVG017 grew at 37°C; no growth at 38°C.
EVG017 had severely reduced sporulation (<1% of wild type), preventing direct insect bioassay.
EVG017 passaged through insect host to restore sporulation, yielding EVG017g with retained thermotolerance and restored conidiation.
Insect bioassays: EVG016 and EVG017g retained pathogenicity but with altered virulence (higher LD50, lower ST50); no mortality at 36°C.
Thermal barriers at 32°C, 36.5°C, and 37.5°C may require multiple or complex mutations; stepwise appearance may reflect mutant frequency changes.
Genetic basis of thermotolerance unknown; need genome sequencing; uncertain if thermal barriers are concrete or pathway artifacts.
Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using Evolugator™; complex phenotypes in non-selected traits but pathogenicity retained.

edges.reason: Hypothesis requires design of Evolugator™ setup.
Failure leads to temperature stabilization strategy.
Defect necessitates revision via insect passage.
Bioassay results lead to analysis of thermal barriers.
Analysis highlights limitations and future work.

must_cover_points: Problem: low thermotolerance of M. anisopliae
Growth limits: 36.5°C (both), 37°C (EVG017 only), no growth at 38°C
Insect bioassays: altered virulence, no mortality at 36°C
Unknown genetic basis, need for genome sequencing
Overall success: first directed evolution of thermotolerance in filamentous fungus
